behaviors.description: Renamed files appear in their own category and are not also counted as a deletion plus an addition.
behaviors.justification: To catch a real failure (rename mis-categorization), the tool must parse actual git data from a repository that contains a renamed file. A pure unit test with mocked git output could hide a failure in how the tool calls git, parses rename detection format, or routes the categorization logic. An Integration test with a fixture repository containing a real renamed file (checked into test fixtures or dynamically created) tests the tool's rename detection against realistic git repository state without requiring subprocess invocation. This catches failures in library usage or output parsing that a mock couldn't expose.
behaviors.test_description: Create a test fixture repository with a committed file that has been renamed (e.g., config.py → settings.py). Run the tool against this repository and verify that the output contains exactly one entry in the 'renamed' category with both the old and new filenames, and that neither file appears in the 'added' or 'deleted' categories.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: The test requires a real repository with a renamed file to be reliable. Mocking git output could pass even if the tool doesn't correctly detect renames. System (actual git commands) could work but adds unnecessary complexity if test repos are pre-built fixtures.

behaviors.description: Diffs that include binary files do not cause the tool to crash; binary files are counted in the output.
behaviors.justification: The real failure mode is the tool crashing or hanging when encountering binary content. A unit test with mocked git output could hide failures in how the tool reads or processes actual binary data. The tool may attempt to decode, parse, or read file content, and only real binary files will expose crashes from invalid UTF-8 sequences, file I/O errors, or library constraints. Integration testing with a fixture repository containing actual binary files (images, executables) ensures the tool handles the actual bytes correctly. This tests the real integration between the tool's file-reading code and the filesystem.
behaviors.test_description: Create a test repository containing multiple binary files (.jpg, .png, .bin, .exe) that have been added, deleted, or modified in the commit history. Run the tool on this repository and verify: (1) the tool completes without crashing or hanging, (2) binary files are correctly counted in their respective categories, and (3) output is valid and parseable regardless of binary content.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Real binary files are essential—a mock of git output would not exercise the tool's binary data handling code paths that could cause crashes or hangs.

behaviors.description: Running the tool on a repository that has no commits yet produces an empty report rather than an error.
behaviors.justification: The real failure is the tool crashing or raising an exception when git has no commits. This is an edge case where git's behavior differs from the normal multi-commit case. A unit test with mocked empty git output could pass even if the tool fails on real empty repositories due to missing branches, refs, or objects. The tool may make assumptions about the HEAD ref or commit count that break when the repository is truly empty. Integration testing with an actual empty git repository (or minimal fixture) verifies the tool handles this corner case correctly.
behaviors.test_description: Initialize a test fixture as an empty git repository with no commits (newly initialized .git directory only). Run the tool against this repository and verify that it completes successfully and outputs an empty report (no added, deleted, renamed, or modified files), without raising exceptions or returning error codes.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: An actual empty repository state is necessary; mocking git output as empty would not catch failures related to missing git objects, refs, or HEAD pointers that only occur in truly empty repositories.

behaviors.description: The counts in the report match the actual change history of the repository being analyzed.
behaviors.justification: This is the core correctness behavior. A unit test cannot verify accuracy without either mocking git output (which tests the tool against a mock, not ground truth) or having a real repository with known, verifiable history. The real failure is incorrect counting—missing files, double-counting, or miscategorization. Integration testing with fixture repositories containing known change sequences (e.g., 'this repo has exactly 5 added, 2 deleted, 1 renamed, 3 modified files in its history') catches counting bugs by comparing tool output to the actual, pre-established repository state. Using pre-built test fixtures avoids the need for subprocess invocation to set up test state.
behaviors.test_description: Create multiple test fixture repositories, each with a different known change history: (a) repo with 5 specific added files, (b) repo with 2 deleted files, (c) repo with 1 renamed file, (d) repo with 3 modified files, and (e) repo with a mix. For each, run the tool and verify the reported counts (total additions, deletions, renames, modifications) match the expected values exactly. Include edge cases: files modified multiple times (should be counted once), and chains of renames.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: The test requires repositories with known, verifiable histories. Pre-built fixtures with checked-in .git directories are sufficient for Integration. Dynamic repository setup with git commands (System) is not necessary if repositories are committed as test data.

behaviors.description: The output is identical whether the changes being analyzed are staged or unstaged.
behaviors.justification: This behavior requires testing the tool's behavior across two different repository states (staged and unstaged changes) that differ only in git's staging index. A unit or integration test cannot reliably set up these states without actually running git commands to stage and unstage changes. The tool may internally check git status or use different code paths for staged vs. unstaged diffs, and mocking these states could miss bugs. The real failure is the tool producing different output based on staging status—to catch this, the test must: (1) set up a repository with uncommitted changes, (2) stage some/all changes using actual git commands, (3) run the tool on both the staged and unstaged states, and (4) verify identical output. This requires subprocess-level interaction with git to manipulate the staging index.
behaviors.test_description: Set up a test repository with uncommitted changes (e.g., modified and added files). Create two test runs: (a) without staging any changes, and (b) with all changes staged using actual git commands. Run the tool on both repository states and verify the output (file counts, categorization, report format) is byte-for-byte identical, regardless of whether the changes are staged or unstaged.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Setting up different git staging states requires running git commands (git add, git reset). This is subprocess-level work, making System the minimum. Integration with mocked git state could miss real bugs in how the tool queries the staging index.
